cards.grammarNote: اليوم (al-yawm) = today. Can open or close the sentence: اليوم أنا مشغول (Today I am busy) or أنا مشغول اليوم (I am busy today). Both are natural.
الآن (al-ān) = now (formal and colloquial). بعدين (baʿdain) = later, very common in colloquial Arabic. The formal equivalent is لاحقاً (lāḥiqan).
قبل (qabl) and بعد (baʿd) can stand alone as adverbs or precede a noun: قبل الغداء (qabl al-ghadāʾ = before lunch), بعد العمل (baʿd al-ʿamal = after work).

cards.front: What day does the Arabic week start on?
cards.grammarNote: The Arabic week runs Sunday (الأحد) through Saturday (السبت). Friday (الجمعة) is the day of communal prayer and is the primary day off in many Arab countries.
الجمعة comes from جمع (jam = gathering/congregation). Friday is the most culturally significant weekday — صلاة الجمعة (ṣalāt al-jumʿa) is the obligatory Friday noon prayer.
To say 'on [day],' use يوم (yawm = day of) before the day name, or simply use the day name with the definite article: الاثنين = on Monday.
أبداً (abadan = never) must be paired with a negated verb: لا أذهب أبداً (lā adhhab abadan = I never go). Alone in response to a question, أبداً means 'absolutely not.'

cards.front: I always drink coffee in the morning.
